results.possibleIdentity: Richard R. Black (1932–2020) was a highly regarded American printmaker and professor of art. He received his BFA from Drake University in 1957 and an MSAE from the University of Wisconsin in 1958. He served as a Professor of Art at Drake University from 1960 until his retirement in 1994. In 1975, he founded and directed the Drake University Biennial Print Symposium, an event that brought nationally recognized artists to Iowa to lecture and demonstrate printmaking techniques. His artistic work, primarily created using the intaglio process, often featured layered textures and patterns that bridged abstraction and recognizable forms. He was a member of the Royal Society of Painter-Printmakers and received various awards, including the Drake Award for excellence in teaching and artistic achievement.

Harry Richard Black (1921–2014) was a prominent American commercial artist, illustrator, and educator based in Dayton, Ohio. He is historically significant for creating the original Mr. Clean mascot for Procter & Gamble in 1957 and for his illustrations of the Smokey Bear character used in wildfire prevention campaigns. Beyond his commercial success, he was a landscape and portrait painter who taught art at both the University of Dayton (starting in 1967) and Sinclair Community College. His career spanned the late 1960s and early 1970s, during which he maintained an active studio in Dayton and was an influential figure in the regional art and education community.

Richard Black is an audio technology consultant and recording engineer who has demonstrated a career-long interest in the intersection of engineering and music. He studied physics and worked in industrial electronics for eight years (including time at British Telecom) while simultaneously pursuing a career as a pianist and producer. Known for having "the ear of a musician and mind of an engineer," he has authored technical papers on cable theory and audio reproduction. This interdisciplinary background aligns with the collaborative spirit of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which sought to pair artists with engineers to explore new means of expression.

results.possibleIdentity: Walter Ungerer (1935–2024) was an American experimental filmmaker and media artist who was a prominent figure in the New York City underground film scene during the 1960s and 1970s. Born in New York City to German immigrants, he studied art and architecture at the Pratt Institute (BFA, 1958) and later earned an MA from Columbia University in 1964. His artistic career began with painting and ceramics before he transitioned to film, releasing his first work, 'The Tasmanian Devil,' in 1965. In 1969, he moved to Vermont where he taught at Goddard College and the University of Vermont, and in 1976 he established Dark Horse Films, a non-profit production company dedicated to experimental narrative films. Ungerer's work is characterized by its transition from 16mm and 35mm film to computer-generated and digital media, reflecting his long-standing interest in the intersection of art and technology. He is frequently associated with the experimental art communities of the late 1960s, and his name appears in historical archives alongside organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), such as in MoMA's Cineprobe series and media art workshops. Notable works from his active period in the 1960s and 70s include 'Meet Me, Jesus' (1966), 'Ubi Est Terram Oobiae?' (1969), 'Keeping Things Whole' (1971), and 'The Animal' (1976). He relocated to Maine in 2003 and continued to produce digital art until his death in 2024.

results.possibleIdentity: Yoshimasa "Yoshi" Wada (1943–2021) was a Japanese-born sound installation artist, composer, and musician who was a central figure in the New York experimental art scene starting in the late 1960s. After studying sculpture at the Kyoto University of Fine Arts, he moved to New York City in 1967 and joined the Fluxus movement in 1968 following a meeting with George Maciunas. Wada is highly regarded for his "interart" practice, which merged sculpture, performance, and music. He was known for creating large-scale, homemade musical instruments from industrial materials such as plumbing pipes, which he called "pipe horns" (including instruments named the Alligator and the Elephantine Crocodile). His musical style was deeply influenced by his studies with minimalist composer La Monte Young and North Indian vocal master Pandit Pran Nath, focusing on sustained drones, high-volume resonances, and overtones. Wada was associated with the downtown New York milieu that fostered collaborations between art and technology, expressing a strong interest in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) events like "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering." His works, such as "Earth Horns with Electric Drone" (1974), are foundational to the history of sound art.

results.possibleIdentity: Nick Curto is a New York-based creative artist, graphic designer, and activist who has been active since the late 1960s and early 1970s. He graduated with a BFA in Advertising and Graphic Design from the Massachusetts College of Art and Design (MassArt) and subsequently moved to New York City to pursue a career in design and the arts. Curto served as an Art Director in the Corporate Communications department of a Fortune 500 company on Wall Street for nine years. He is a prominent figure in the LGBTQ+ arts community, having served as the founding president of All Out Arts, Inc. and the Fresh Fruit Festival. His design work includes the graphic logo for the first local PBS program dedicated to the lesbian and gay communities (aired on WNET in 1974) and the 'Equal Logo' for The Equality Project. In the late 1970s, he was a spokesperson for the National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights. Curto also explores the intersection of art, science, and technology through his podcast, 'Nick Curto Presents: Connecting the Dots,' and his role as Co-Founder and Director of Disclosure Network New York (DNNY), where he discusses topics such as physics, DNA nanotechnology, and extraterrestrial phenomena. This multidisciplinary interest aligns with the ethos of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which promoted collaborations between artists and scientists.

results.possibleIdentity: Ron Dubren (often misspelled or referred to as Ron Duhen) is an American artist, toy inventor, and game designer who was active in the New York avant-garde art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known in the commercial world as the co-inventor of the 'Tickle Me Elmo' toy, but his early career was rooted in experimental art and technology. Dubren moved to New York City in 1965 and became involved with the community of artists, engineers, and scientists associated with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the Annual Avant-Garde Festival of New York. In 1970, his experimental video work titled 'The Magic Box' was featured in a group exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) dedicated to emerging video art. Throughout the 1970s, he participated in various multimedia and performance-based events, including multiple editions of the Annual Avant-Garde Festival (such as the 11th festival in 1974). His work often bridged the gap between psychology (he holds a PhD in Clinical Psychology from NYU, 1975), technology, and play. Later in his career, he focused on game and toy design, collaborating with engineers like Greg Hyman to create interactive electronic products.

results.possibleIdentity: Dolores H. "Dilly" Schapiro (1930–2022) was a New York-based environmental designer, architectural staff member, and prominent patron of the arts. She was active in the New York design and art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s, which aligns with the period of peak activity for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Schapiro earned a bachelor's degree in environmental design from the Parsons School of Design in 1973 and also attended the Columbia University School of Architecture. In the 1970s, she worked for New York architectural firms and served on the staff of the West Side Highway Project, a major urban planning initiative. Beyond her professional career, she was a dedicated arts enthusiast and a longtime patron of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Her marriage to Dr. Rolf Schapiro further connected her to prominent circles in New York and Pittsburgh, where she spent her final years.
